name: Trigonometric Functions — Angles, Radians and the Unit Circle
whatIsIt: In Class 11, trigonometry grows up: angles are measured not just in DEGREES but in RADIANS (π radians = 180°), and sine, cosine and tangent become FUNCTIONS defined for every angle using the UNIT CIRCLE (a circle of radius 1). A point on the unit circle at angle θ has coordinates (cos θ, sin θ). Key identity: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.
whyNeeded: Radians and the unit circle unlock waves, circular motion, oscillations, and all of calculus with trig. Physics, engineering, music and signal processing describe repeating things with these functions.
realLifeUses: Circular motion — a wheel or a planet's orbit
Modelling tides and seasons that repeat

vocabulary.meaning: An angle measure where π radians = 180° (a full circle is 2π radians).
A circle of radius 1 centred at the origin; a point at angle θ is (cos θ, sin θ).
Always true — it comes from the circle's radius being 1 (Pythagoras).
Repeats every 360° (2π): sin and cos come back to the same value.

story.title: The Ferris Wheel Angle
story.text: On a Ferris wheel, Aditya asks how high his seat is at the top-side. The guide says the seat's height follows the sine of the turning angle. 'Half a turn is 180°, or π radians,' she explains. At 90° (a quarter turn, π/2 radians) the seat is at its highest, and sin 90° = 1. 'The wheel keeps repeating every full turn — that's why sine is called periodic.'
story.extractedProblem: Half a turn is 180°. How many radians is that?
story.answerInStory: π radians — because π radians = 180° by definition.

shapes.label: unit circle: point at angle θ is (cos θ, sin θ)

visual.caption: On the unit circle (radius 1), the angle θ gives a point (cos θ, sin θ). Because the radius is 1, sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.

teachingGallery.title: Degrees and radians
teachingGallery.note: π radians = 180°. Multiply degrees by π/180 to get radians.

shapes.label: sin θ = opp/hyp, cos θ = adj/hyp

examples.caption: In a right triangle sin θ and cos θ are ratios; on the unit circle they are coordinates.
A full circle is 360° = 2π radians; a quarter is 90° = π/2.
Sine is periodic — it repeats its values every full turn.

standardMethod.summary: Convert between degrees and radians with π = 180°. Use the unit circle: (cos θ, sin θ). Use sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 and known values (sin 30° = 1/2, etc.).
steps: Degrees → radians: multiply by π/180. Radians → degrees: multiply by 180/π.
On the unit circle, the point at angle θ is (cos θ, sin θ).
Use standard values: sin 0° = 0, sin 30° = 1/2, sin 45° = 1/√2, sin 90° = 1 (cos is the mirror).
Apply the identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 to find one ratio from the other.

workedExamples.problem: How many degrees is π radians?
steps: So π radians is exactly half a circle.

commonMistakes.mistake: Treating an angle in radians as if it were degrees (or vice versa).
commonMistakes.fix: π radians = 180°. Always convert first: to go from degrees to radians multiply by π/180.

commonMistakes.mistake: Writing sin θ + cos θ = 1 instead of sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.
commonMistakes.fix: The identity uses SQUARES: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 (from the radius-1 circle and Pythagoras).

easy.q: How many degrees are in a full circle?
π radians equals how many degrees?

questions.q: A full circle is how many radians in terms of π? Write the number multiplying π (2π → 2).
hintLadder: The number in front of π.

questions.q: On the unit circle, the point at angle θ is…
hintLadder: x = cos θ, y = sin θ.

teachBackPrompt: Draw a unit circle for a grown-up and show that the point at angle θ is (cos θ, sin θ). Explain that π radians = 180°, that sin 90° = 1, and why sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.

revisionCard.summary: Radians: π rad = 180°, full circle = 2π (360°). Unit circle point at θ = (cos θ, sin θ). Identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 (squares!). Standard values: sin 30° = 1/2, sin 45° = 1/√2, sin 90° = 1. sin and cos repeat every 360° — they are periodic.
